coredata.dc:title: In-situ fabrication of N-doped carbon dots on novel blue-emitting Y2TiWO8: Ce3+ nanophosphor for thermal sensing and fingerprint detection applications
coredata.dc:creator: Narwal, Sudha
Sura, Ankoor
Singh, A.
Azam, Mohammad
Alam, Mahboob
Duhan, S.
Nain, Sonia
coredata.dc:description: A series of N-doped Carbon Dots @ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 blue-emitting nanophosphors were obtained by the sol-gel assisted hydrothermal route for exploring the optical thermometry and latent fingerprint visualisation. X-Ray Diffraction, morphology, and PL spectra analysed the synthesised nanophosphors. Excited by 355 nm, the Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor shows a broad blue emission band at 424 nm, resulting from the electric dipole transition 5d → 4f of Ce3+ and the N-doped Carbon Dots @ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor shows the blue emission band at 431 nm. A dramatic increase by a factor of 342.82 was observed in PL intensity when nitrogen doped carbon dots were embedded in the Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor. The enhanced PL intensity can be attributed to the FRET mechanism. Compared to its emission intensity at room temperature, the nanophosphor maintains 85 % of its intensity even at 400 K, demonstrating outstanding thermal stability. The N-doped Carbon Dots@ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 exhibit high colour purity of 93.25 %. Further, the nanophosphor can be potentially used for optical thermometry with a wide temperature range from 300 K to 460 K. The latent fingerprint developed by N-doped Carbon Dots@ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor presents excellent selectivity and high contrast. Under UV irradiation, latent fingerprints' level 1–3 structural features may be easily recognised.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of N-doped Carbon Dots @ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 blue-emitting nanophosphors were obtained by the sol-gel assisted hydrothermal route for exploring the optical thermometry and latent fingerprint visualisation. X-Ray Diffraction, morphology, and PL spectra analysed the synthesised nanophosphors. Excited by 355 nm, the Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor shows a broad blue emission band at 424 nm, resulting from the electric dipole transition 5d → 4f of Ce3+ and the N-doped Carbon Dots @ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor shows the blue emission band at 431 nm. A dramatic increase by a factor of 342.82 was observed in PL intensity when nitrogen doped carbon dots were embedded in the Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor. The enhanced PL intensity can be attributed to the FRET mechanism. Compared to its emission intensity at room temperature, the nanophosphor maintains 85 % of its intensity even at 400 K, demonstrating outstanding thermal stability. The N-doped Carbon Dots@ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 exhibit high colour purity of 93.25 %. Further, the nanophosphor can be potentially used for optical thermometry with a wide temperature range from 300 K to 460 K. The latent fingerprint developed by N-doped Carbon Dots@ Ce3+-doped Y2TiWO8 nanophosphor presents excellent selectivity and high contrast. Under UV irradiation, latent fingerprints' level 1–3 structural features may be easily recognised.

ce:section.ce:para: The phosphors are photoluminescent materials having multiple applications in different fields, such as temperature sensing [1–6], forensic sciences [7,8], and anti-counterfeiting [8–10]. Nowadays, nanothermometers have advanced applications in research, technology, healthcare, and commercial applications due to their unique spectrum features and high chemical and physical stability. Rare-Earth doped inorganic nanoparticles are the most intriguing choice for temperature monitoring over a broad range of temperatures. Commonly used spectroscopic temperature-dependent metrics for luminescence thermometers include FWHM and emission lifetime [5,6,11–14]. In forensic sciences, discovering latent fingerprints has traditionally been done using ninhydrin, cyanoacrylate fuming, and powder dusting procedures. Among these methods, powder dusting has become popular due to its less complexity, reliability, and ease of use. However, traditional powder types like metal and magnetic powder have been replaced by Rare Earth (RE) doped phosphors. These phosphors offer better contrast, selectivity, and less background activity, making them preferred over traditional powders [7,8].

Metal oxides have been effectively employed as a phosphor host framework for hosting fluorescent Ln3+-ions due to their robust photochemical properties, outstanding thermal and photochemical firmness, appropriate phonon energies, high dielectric permittivity (j), broadband gaps, and physical stabilities [15–27]. Tungstate has also been shown to be an excellent metal oxide due to its strong chemical sustenance and high average refraction coefficient, which facilitate energy-efficient transfer from its host matrices to the specific states of doping ions. Tungstate help improve luminescence intensity and colour purity as they are highly efficient in absorbing near-ultraviolet light and transferring energy to the RE3+ ion emission centre [28–30]. The selection of good dopant ions is also crucial. Among RE3+ ions, only cerium (Ce3+) ions and europium (Eu3+) ions have a spin-allowed 4f–5d transition. Due to their useful applications in white LEDs, dosimetry, and photonic applications, Ce3+ ions have recently risen to prominence as dopant materials. As reported in the literature, phosphors doped with Ce3+ ions typically exhibit significant and broad absorption near the UV region, which is greatly dependent on the host matrix [14,17,31,32]. The phosphors CaSr2Al2O6: Ce3+ [31], Ca2Al2SiO7: Ce3+ [32], and SrZrSi2O7: Ce3+ [17] all exhibit blue emission and have been used in various applications, but there is little study on efficient Ce3+ - doped blue-emitting phosphors.

Furthermore, because of their surface flaws, poor molar absorptivity, narrow absorption band, and other factors, lanthanide ion-doped nanomaterials frequently exhibit reduced luminescence quantum efficiency. Therefore, to overcome these defects, there is a need to improve surface defects and luminescence quantum efficiency. The quantum dots (QDs) can act as sensitisers and surface modifiers, as they can accomplish the purpose of enhanced photoluminescence (PL) by tuning the optical band gap of the particles of the desired materials. Quantum carbon dots (CDs) have gained popularity due to their exceptional qualities, such as lower toxicities, high dependability, and luminous brightness. This has led to a surge in their use across various exciting applications. Incorporating CDs into phosphors improved their morphology and luminous efficiency [33–38]. Recently, some studies on composites of CDs and RE-doped luminescent materials have been conducted, and in YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3: Eu3+, Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites, the Fӧrster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) from CDs to Eu3+ boosted the photoluminescence intensity [39]. Additionally, the CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ showed improved PL intensity than the YBO3:Eu3+ due to the FRET mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ [40].

Herein, we explored novel, highly stable, efficient blue-emitting phosphor nanomaterial. As far as we know, the compounds with the general formula LnzM4+N6+Os are untouched, and no study has been done on their nanocomposites with carbon dots. The unique N-doped CDs@ Y1.98TiWO8:(0.02) Ce3+ blue-emitting nanophosphor was fabricated using a sol-gel assisted hydrothermal process at a low operating temperature (900 °C) compared to previously established solid-state reactions [41]. The as-prepared nanophosphors can be used for fields like temperature sensing using optical thermometry, forensic sciences (detecting latent fingerprints), solid-state lighting, and anti-counterfeiting. Analysis of the nanophosphors' structure and morphology, elemental content, PL characteristics, temperature, and concentration quenching are studied the current work. Additionally, N-doped CDs@ Y1.98TiWO8:(0.02) Ce3+ nanophosphors' CIE coordinates, colour purity (CP), and correlated colour temperature (CCT) were examined. Herein, we have discussed temperature sensing and the detection of latent fingerprint applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Y2TiWO8 and Y2-xTiWO8: xCe3+(0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nanophosphors
ce:section.ce:para: The sol-gel method synthesised the Y2TiWO8 and Y2-xTiWO8: xCe3+(0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nanophosphors. The primary components utilised were of high purity Y(NO3)3.5H2O (with 99.9 % purity from Sigma Aldrich), Ce(NO3)3.6H2O (with 99.9 % purity from Sigma Aldrich), Ti(OC4H9)4 and Na2WO4.2H2O (with 99 % purity from Sigma Aldrich). In the 20 ml of deionised water, the stoichiometric amount of the nitrate of Yttrium was dissolved. A transparent solution (solution X) was obtained by continuously heating and stirring the two-component solution. 20 ml of deionised water (solution Y) was combined with weighted Na2WO4.2H2O. Solution Z was created by measuring 40 ml of pure ethanol, mixing it with 10 ml of deionised water, and then adding Ti(OC4H9)4 in a stoichiometric amount. Then, solution X was fused in solution Z, and lastly, solution Y was gradually added, a drop at a time, and a white sol was immediately evident. The resultant mixture was constantly agitated for 2 h while being kept at a temperature of 75 °C. The wet sol was then baked in an oven for 12 h at 80 °C. The dried sol-gel was then pre-sintered for 5 h at 500 °C, followed by 4 h of annealing at 900 °C. The sample was thoroughly ground after sintering to prepare for the subsequent characterisations. For synthesis of Y2-xTiWO8: xCe3+(0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) nanophosphors, in the 20 ml of deionised water, the stoichiometric amount of the nitrates of Cerium and Yttrium were dissolved and followed the same steps as above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped CDs@Y1.98TiWO8: (0.02) Ce3+ nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 10 ml of distilled water was combined with 2.0 g of urea and 1.0 g of citric acid. The mixture was stirred for 10 min. Then, 0.05 g of the nanophosphor, dissolved in 3 ml of water, was added. The above solution was thoroughly combined by agitation for an additional 10 min, then transferred to a 100 ml autoclave and allowed to react for 8 h at 200 °C. The sample was synthesised by rinsing the cooled solution with ethanol and centrifuging it three times at 4500 rpm. Since the N-doped CDs are impregnated on the Y1.98TiWO8: (0.02) Ce3+ (YTWO: Ce3+) surface, there was no need for dialysis. Before further characterisation and application, the final sample was dried at 80 °C for 6 h. Furthermore, producing vast quantities of these nanocomposite materials is simple and doesn't require a drawn-out procedure.

Utilising a 3rd generation Empyrean, Malvern Panalytical XRD with Cu K-alpha radiation (λ = 0.154 nm), the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of obtained nanophosphors are characterised. The 2θ range of the recorded XRD data was between 10° to 70°. The FE-SEM & EDS technique characterises the morphology and composition of elements by using high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (7610F Plus/JEOL) coupled with energy dispersive X-rays (EDX) and HR-TEM technique by using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy TECNAI 200 Kv TEM (Fei, Electron Optics). Photoluminescence spectra and lifetime decay were recorded using QuantaMaster 8450-22 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba) coupled with a Xe lamp (450 W) as an exciting light source. STED microscopy (LEICA STELLARIS) for detection of latent fingerprints in brightfield and under 405 nm UV excitation.

Considering a series of Y2-xM4+M6+O8:xRE3+ (M4+ = Si4+, Ge4+; M6+ = W6+, Mo6+; RE3+ = Eu3+, Dy3+, Sm3+) [42–44], compounds have crystal structures that are alike, here, Fig. 1 (a) displays the typical XRD patterns of YTWO, YTWO: xCe3+ (0.02≤ x ≤ 0.12) sol-gel process prepared nanophosphors annealed at 900 °C for 4 h. Fig. 2 shows the XRD pattern of YTWO: Ce3+ and CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ synthesised by the sol-gel and hydrothermal methods. It is possible to prepare the pristine phase of YTWO using a sol-gel method at just 900 °C, which is substantially far less than the processing temperature of 1400 °C required for its synthesis via a solid-state technique, as shown in Fig. 1(a), which indicates all peaks of diffraction patterns of the resulting samples [41]. Shifting of the peak at 2θ = 29.86° is observed, indicating the doping of rare earth ions (Ce3+) in the YTWO host lattice {Fig. 1(b) }. Further, the d-spacing of the nanocomposite material is 0.30 nm, which is located in the (1 1 2) plane of YTWO: Ce3+ which is also confirmed by HR-TEM in section 3.2.3. For the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+, a hump at 2θ = 20° could be ascribed to the CDs (Fig. 2(a)) [45]. The XRD data of synthesised CDs in Fig. 2(b) shows a hump at 2θ value of 20° which can be ascribed to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon. Additionally, it was shown that the peak intensities were stronger with the introduction of CDs, and the FWHM (full width at half-maxima) was smaller. Due to introduction of CDs into lattice, some redox reactions are taking place which increase the crystallinity of host matrix [46].

The ionic radius of Ce3+ (1.14 Å) is similar to that of Y3+ (1.02 Å), so it is reasonable that the Ce3+ ion will be integrated into the YTWO host lattice by replacing Y3+ion. We can compute the radius percentage difference (Dr) using the formula below to verify this supposition [17]:
(1)
D r
= 100 % ×
[
R m
( CN )
−
R d
( CN )
R m
( CN )
]

The substituted ion's ionic radius is Rm (CN), while the doped ion's ionic radius is Rd (CN). The doped ion's radius should not differ more than 30 % from the substituted ion's radius. The Dr of Y3+ and Ce3+ in the calculation is 12.87 %. Therefore, it is proven that the Y3+ ion is replaced by the Ce3+ ion. Scherrer's formula, which is as follows, can be used to determine the particle sizes of the YTWO: Ce3+ sample [47].
(2)
D =
K λ
β
cos
θ
where K (=0.94) stands for Scherrer Constant, λ stands for the X-ray wavelength (0.15405 nm), D stands for the average crystallite size, β stands for the FWHM of an observed peak, and θ is the diffraction angle. YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor has an average crystallite size that has been determined to be 14.90 nm. The Williamson-Hall (W–H) plot for CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphors is exhibited in Supplementary Information Fig. S1. The W–H analysis is used to calculate crystallite size and lattice strain. According to the calculations, the average crystallite size is 11.55 nm. The size difference can be due to the presence of micro strain in the crystal structure caused by Ce3+ ions.

Firstly, the FE-SEM technique was used to understand the morphology of the undoped (c.f. supplementary information S2), Ce3+- doped (c.f. supplementary information S3), and CDs-embedded Ce3+ doped YTWO nanoparticles. The dried nanophosphors were covered with gold sputtering coating before being subjected to FE-SEM examination at various scales. The nanoparticles of undoped YTWO, with an average particle size of 0.145 μm, seem uneven and slightly aggregated. Strikingly, the incorporation of Ce3+ ions positively affect morphology as Ce3+- doped YTWO nanoparticles seem smoothly diffused, proportionate, and smaller, with an average particle size of 0.135 μm.

Moreover, the size of nanoparticles of Ce3+ doped YTWO decreased further when they were treated under hydrothermal conditions for synthesising CDs@YTWO: (0.02) Ce3+. As can be seen from Fig. 3 (a) and 3(b) , the nanoparticles are more crystalline and smaller in size, with an average particle size of 0.124 μm {Fig. 3 (c)}, as compared to undoped and Ce3+ doped YTWO and the size of embedded CDs nanoparticles is between 3 and 5 nm, with an average size of 4.35 nm {Fig. 3 (d)}. Also, the crystals arranged themselves into the formation of a flower-like shape {as shown in the top right corner of Fig. 3(a)}. It must also be noted that the CDs are present on every layer (petal) of the flower-like CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+. Further, it can be said that the SEM image resembles with flower decorated with tiny water droplets. The improved crystallinity of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ is also supported by the XRD result {Fig. 2(a)} showing peaks with high intensity and reduced FWHM. These results also reveal that carbon dots act as surface modifiers.

Further, the composition of elements of the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ is shown in Fig. 3 (e). EDS peaks confirm that Yttrium (Y), Titanium (Ti), Tungsten (W), Oxygen (O), Cerium (Ce), Carbon (C), and Nitrogen (N) elements are present in the products, and none of the additional peaks are obtained; hence, the products are pristine nanophosphors. The CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor elemental mapping images display numerous elements in various hues, demonstrating that these elements are located equally in the sample {Fig. 3 (f)}. It means that the carbon dots are distributed evenly across the host matrix. It is also said that Ce3+ ions successfully substituted the Y3+ ions in the YTWO matrix lattice. These findings reveal the successful synthesis of the desired nanophosphors.

The HR-TEM technique was further used for analysing the morphology of undoped {c.f. supplementary information S4 (a)}, Ce3+-doped {c.f. supplementary information S4 (b)}, and CDs-embedded Ce3+ - doped YTWO nanoparticles. The samples were made in ethanol, underwent sonication for 10 min, deposited on the copper grid, and dried in a hygroscopic environment at standard ambient temperature. The TEM images of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The average interplanar distance of the nanocomposite material is 0.30 nm, which is located in the (1 1 2) plane of YTWO: Ce3+ (inner part of the green circle in the inset of Fig. 4(a)). The large number of carboxylic acid components that CDs typically have at their surface gives them a high affinity for water. They are ideal for further integration with other polymeric, organic, inorganic, or biologically active substances. In Fig. 4(a), tight adherence of the individual CD to the surface of YTWO: Ce3+ can be seen, which is clearly explained by the binding between carboxylic acid components and YTWO: Ce3+, thus distinguishing the boundaries between CD and the surface of YTWO: Ce3+, could not be done. Further, the lattice spacing between lattice planes is around 0.25 nm (as shown in the red circle in the inset of Fig. 4(a)), and these are the diffraction planes of carbon dots. The black dots in Fig. 4(b) are the embedded carbon dots on the surface of YTWO: Ce3+. Fig. 4(c) shows the SAED pattern of CDs @ YTWO: Ce3+. The bright dots in the SAED pattern manifest the polycrystalline structure of the prepared nanophosphors.

With a [Xe]4f1 electronic configuration, the Ce3+ ion exhibits a single 4f-4f absorption, as shown by the Dieke diagram in Fig. 5 (a), which can be attributed to the shift from 2F5/2 to 2F7/2 levels that are triggered by spin-orbit coupling. Additionally, Ce3+ ions can be promoted into the [Xe]4f05 d1 electronic structure. The free ion of [Xe]4f05 d1 exhibits absorption from the ground state 4f to the 5d excited state. Furthermore, Ce3+ exhibits 5d-4f luminosity from the UV to visible range in Ce3+-doped materials with potent 4f-5d absorption [48].

The PL excitation and emission spectrum of YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor is shown respectively in Fig. 5(b) and (c)
. The excitation spectrum is resolved into five bands with 339, 355, 367, 371, and 393 nm peaks. These five bands are consistent with the five energy levels of the crystal's 5d excited states of Ce3+ [32]. The peak at 355 nm was more intense than other peaks in the excitation spectrum. Hence, the 355 nm wavelength was selected as the excitation wavelength for further studies. Steady-state excitation at 355 nm in the UV excitation region (300–400 nm) produces broadband luminescence (asymmetric blue emission ranging from 370 to 600 nm) which is centred at 424 nm, being due to dipole allowed 5d → 4f transition of Ce3+ ions at different sites. UV light can effectively excite this nanophosphor, as evidenced by the broad excitation band from the electron transfer of the Ce3+ ions from the 2F5/2 ground state to the multiple excited 5d states broken down by the crystal field. The luminescence band has a tail that transitions to the 2 F5/2 and 2 F7/2 ground state of Ce3+.

The emission spectra of the Ce3+ ions may be fitted into two different peaks for 2F7/2 and 2F5/2 levels. The intensity vs. wavelength PLE spectra is insufficient for analysing the separation between Ce3+ ion levels (2F7/2 and 2F5/2). As a result, wavelength intervals are expressed in energy units. The Jacobian transformation is employed for this conversion [49]. The following equation can be used to convert wavelength data to energy (3):
(3)
E =
h c
λ
Where E denotes energy, h means Planck's constant, c represents light speed, and λ represents wavelength. It should be outlined that the wavelength can be converted into an energy scale using the formula mentioned above. However, because the relationship between energy and wavelength is inverse, the observed signal measurements in the wavelength scale do not have a constant relationship with the energy scale. If the observed signal measurements are thought of as a function Φ(λ), then the issue above can be resolved by applying the following formula [50,51]:
(4)
Φ
( E )
dE = Φ
( λ )
d λ

From above equations:

)

(5)
Or Φ

This component is referred to as the Jacobian Transformation [49,52]. Fig. 5 (d) displays the energy scale of the Gaussian fitting curve for YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor. The peak is split at 2.73 and 2.95 eV due to the shift of the 5d charged levels to 2F7/2 and 2F5/2 states. The energy variance among both peaks is 0.22 eV, close to the theoretical energy difference between levels 2F7/2 and 2F5/2 of about 0.248 eV.

To enhance the luminescence intensity of YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor, the fluorescent N-doped Carbon Dots (CDs) were introduced into YTWO: Ce3+ to form CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposite. The PL excitation and emission spectrum of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposite are presented in Fig. 6 (a) and 6(b) , respectively. The excitation spectrum is resolved into five bands with 339, 355, 368, 373, and 396 nm peaks. At the excitation of 355 nm, the nanophosphor shows a broadband emission ranging from 370 to 600 nm centred at 431 nm. Monitored at 431 nm, the nanophosphor exhibits a stronger excitation band from 300 to 400 nm centring at 355 nm.

The PL intensity of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposite is significantly higher than that of YTWO: Ce3+. The FRET between CDs and Ce3+ ions could explain this occurrence. There are three fundamental requirements for FRET in theory: To begin, donor and acceptor molecules must be near together (usually within 1–10 nm). This condition has been confirmed by SEM images {Fig. 3(a)} and TEM images {Fig. 4(a)} of the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposite, which demonstrates the CDs' tight adherence on the surface of YTWO: Ce3+. Second, the acceptor's spectrum of absorbance must cross over with the donor's luminescence emission spectrum. The energy transfer occurs in the spectral cross-section region shown in Fig. 6 (c). Finally, the donor and acceptor transition dipole orientations must generally be parallel [40]. Through the dipole-dipole interaction between the transition dipole moments of the acceptor and donor, FRET can transfer energy. This interaction is covered in more detail in section 3.3.6. After absorbing the light, the donor (CDs) is elevated to the excited state, which opens the door to two possibilities. It will either non-radiatively transmit energy to the acceptor (Ce3+) or radiatively relax by fluorescence.

The mechanism of luminescence enhancement can be explained by the fact that when CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ was excited at a wavelength of 355 nm, this caused transition of electrons in Ce3+ from the 2F5/2 to different components of the excited 5d energy level, while electrons in CDs transitioned from HOMO to LUMO with the aid of absorbed photon energy, as shown in Fig. 6 (d). The excited electrons were subsequently relaxed to the core and surface state energies and then transitioned to the 5D0-2 energy levels of Ce3+ through the FRET mechanism. The electrons are transferred to 5D0 through non-radiative relaxation and to the ground states of Ce3+ (2F7/2 and 2F5/2) to produce blue emission [53,54]. The CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposite also demonstrates a broad excitation spectrum with a range of 300–400 nm and a major peak at 355 nm, which corresponds with the emission wavelength (350–420 nm) of the currently popular LED chip. Consequently, a wider variety of applications in the field of luminescent materials are made possible for it [40].

Table 1 lists the comparative data of enhanced PL of different CDs nanocomposite materials. The emission intensity of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposites increased 342.82 times that of YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor {Fig. 6 (e)}. The change in FWHM value indicates that the crystallinity of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ has improved. This change was apparently due to fewer positive and negative imperfections in the nanophosphor due to CDs inclusion, which resulted in an oxidation/reduction effect.

Further, the decay lifetime of the as-prepared nanophosphors was calculated. Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show decay curves of YTWO: Ce3+ and CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphors (λexc = 355 nm), respectively. The following equation illustrates how the luminescence decay curves are nicely matched to the double exponent functional [40]:
(6) I = A1 exp(-t/τ1) + A2 exp(-t/τ2)
where I is emission intensity at time t, A1, A2 are constants, and τ1, τ2 are the double exponential components of the decay time. In luminous materials, the energy transfer phenomena frequently result in the double exponential decay behaviour. The following equation can be used to get the average lifetime (τavg) [40]:
(7) τavg= (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2) / (A1τ1+ A2τ2)
and the values are 2.607 ms and 3.474 ms for YTWO: Ce3+ and CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposite, respectively. The FRET between CDs and Ce3+ ions is also explained by the great lifetime of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ compared to YTWO: Ce3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CIE coordinates, colour purity, and CCT
ce:section.ce:para:
Luminous colour plays a crucial role in evaluating the performance of nanophosphors. Light of any colour can be visually represented as (x, y) coordinates on the CIE chromaticity diagram. Based on their emission spectra, The colour coordinates of the samples were calculated by CIE1931 software. In the chromaticity diagram, the CIE coordinates of YTWO: Ce3+ and CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ are presented in Fig. 8 (a) and (b), respectively. One of the points can be used to represent any one of the colours in the chromaticity diagram. The higher the colour purity of the nanophosphor, the greater the proximity of the nanophosphor position to the boundary. The CIE coordinates of the YTWO: Ce3+ and CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ samples are all near the blue region's edge. Further, to calculate the colour purity, the following formula can be used [61]:
(8)
Colour
Purity =

where (x, y), (xd, yd), and (xi, yi) are, in turn, the nanophosphors' CIE coordinates, the dominating wavelength, and the white light's standard illuminant, respectively. The colour purity is up to 93.25 % for the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ sample.

The McCamy empirical formula (9) was used to determine the Correlated Colour Temperature (CCT) values [62,63].
(9) CCT = − 499n3 + 3525n2 − 6823.3n + 5520.33
Where n =
x −
x e
y −
y e
.

Here, (x, y) denotes the chromaticity coordinate of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor and (xe, ye) are the epicentre chromaticity coordinates, with xe = 0.332 and ye = 0.186, respectively. The obtained nanophosphor CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ has a CCT of 2093.18 K. CCTs below 4000 K are classified as having a warm light glance. As a result, the produced nanophosphor can be beneficial for applications such as UltraViolet-Light Emitting Diodes [17].

Fig. 9 (a) and 9(b) show the excitation and emission spectra of YTWO: xCe3+ (0.02 ≤ x ≤ 0.12) samples, respectively, to explore the effect of Ce3+ doping level on emission intensity. With increasing doping concentration, the emission peaks aren't influenced much. The emission intensity increases first and drops, peaking at 0.06 Ce3+ doping amount. This condition occurs because when the concentration of Ce3+ advances, the gap between Ce3+ ions decrease. Hence, the Ce3+ ions come in contact with each other. It unfolds by non-radiative exchange of energy between Ce3+ ions, and it is known as concentration quenching. To assess the type of contact, the Rc (critical transfer distance) of Ce3+ ions in the YTWO: xCe3+ nanophosphor can be calculated using the formula below [64]:
(10)
R c
= 2
[
3 V
4 π
X c
N
]
1 / 3

N is the number of substitutable ions in this case. Xc denotes optimum concentration, V denotes cell volume, and Rc is calculated to be 16.670 Å. Rc is more than 5 Å, indicating that electric multipolar interaction induced concentration quenching. The correlation between log I/x and log x in the YTWO: Ce3+ sample was determined, and the corresponding graph is shown in Fig. 9 (c). Dexter's energy transfer formula can establish a particular kind of multipole energy exchange mechanism. It is detailed below [51]:
(11)
I x
= K
[ 1 + β
( x )
Q 3
]
− 1
Where x defines the amount of doped Ce3+ ions, I define the emission magnitude, and K and β are constants. The interactions are dipole-dipole, dipole-quadrupole, and quadrupole-quadrupole for Q = 6, 8, or 10. The slope produced by linear fitting of log x and log I/x is approximately −2.04217, and the estimated value of Q = 6.12651(∼6). As a result, it suggests that the quenching mode of Ce3+ is a dipole-dipole interaction [50,65].

The PL spectra of CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ analysed in the range of temperature 300–460 K are shown in Fig. 10 . The different colour profiles indicate different emission intensities. Changes in the temperature did not alter the peak's position but lowered the nanophosphor's intensity. Therefore, it can be confirmed that the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor has superior thermal stability. After prolonged use, the temperature of LED bulbs will rise. As a result, it is vital to explore the impact of temperature on phosphor emission characteristics. Fig. 11 indicates that at 400 K, the emission intensity remained 85 % of that at 300 K. As a result, the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor with outstanding thermal stability may be a viable LED phosphor.

Many researchers proposed that the temperature-enhanced population of non-radiative surface (trap/defect) state channels is responsible for the thermal quenching of CDs emission. An Arrhenius plot of the integrated PL intensities could be used to quantitatively analyse the relationship between non-radiative trapping and temperature using the equation given below [7]:
(12) I(T) = I0/(1+Ae−Ea/kT)

I(T) is the PL intensity that depends on temperature (T); A is an indeterminate constant; I0 is the PL intensity at the beginning temperature; and k is the Boltzmann constant. The relationship between ln[{I0/I}-1] and 1/(kT) is shown in Fig. 11 (a). After linear fitting, it can be determined that Ea = 0.22 eV.

The Ea symbol represents the activation energy for thermal quenching. The resistance of a phosphor against the thermal quenching of luminescence is generally correlated with the activation energy for the thermal quenching process. The resistance increases with increasing activation energy.

An illustration of the configurational coordinate diagram schematic is provided in Fig. 11(a) in order to clarify the concept of temperature-dependent phosphor emission. The excitation process is shown by the vertical blue lines with arrows. The radiative and non-radiative emission processes are shown by the yellow and red dotted lines with arrows, respectively. The ground state electrons transferred to the excited state under the excitation of 355 nm light. Most electrons reverted to the ground state over route 1 (M-N-O; M − N is the non-radiatively relaxed process and N–O is the radiative process) at room temperature. But when the temperature rises, the majority of electrons absorb activation energy. If the electron energy is at least as high as the Ea value, i.e., the P point, the activators descend to the ground state via route 2 (M–P–L) without emitting radiation.

As a result, a great Ea value indicates a huge energy barrier for thermal quenching, making it challenging for electrons to navigate back to the ground state via route 2. As a result, when raising the temperature, there is not a significant loss of activation energy, suggesting strong thermal stability. Consequently, the high Ea (=0.22eV) obtained for the Y1.98TiWO8:(0.02) Ce3+ phosphor in this work demonstrated its excellent thermal stability.

Moreover, temperature-dependent shifts in the intensity of emissions suggest that it could be used in fluorescence intensity thermometry, a facile optical approach for non-invasive temperature assessment. Extensive scans, fast reactions in biological fluorescent tags, precise estimation of surroundings temperature in healthcare facilities, and non-invasive and direct temperature evaluation of an impassable object are all possible with optical thermometry. The high Ea value of 0.22 eV implies that the CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ are highly stable and, thus, can be used for optical thermometry applications.

To ensure accurate results when using optical thermometry, it's important to consider the Sr value. This value indicates the level of sensitivity of the thermometer to temperature changes and shows how the thermometric parameter changes per Kelvin. With the help of the below-defined formula, Sr can be calculated [6]:
(13)
S r
=
1
F W H M
d
F W H M
d t
× 100 %

The Sr readings as a measure of temperature have been determined using Eq. (13) and are shown in Fig. 11 (b). The determined Sr values increase with temperature, with a maximum Sr value of 12 × 10−2 % K −1 at 460 K, which can be compared to other luminous sensors according to the temperature-dependent FWHM value in Table 2 .

A 28-year-old female provided a fresh fingerprint. The finger was scrubbed with soap and then dried for some time. The cleansed finger was pressed on a smooth surface after being softly tapped on the face. The latent fingerprint impression was concealed with CDs @ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor, and any leftover nanophosphor was removed using a camel hair brush. Using a fluorescence microscope, coated prints were recorded under UV light irradiation and in a brightfield to check the effectiveness of nanophosphor for latent fingerprint development. The white colour of the nanopowder stood out firmly against both coloured and dark surfaces. With insignificant surroundings noise, the latent fingerprints of nanophosphor exhibited ridges characteristics.

The uneven patterns of valleys and ridges are the most noticeable structural characteristics of fingerprints. Level 1 (the ridge patterns), Level 2 (detailing spots), and Level 3 (local design of pore ridge margins) are the different levels of description that can be applied to ridge details. Level-1 features like the left loop (1) and the ridges (5) are used to classify fingerprints but are lacking for precise identification. Level 2 contains the ridge ending (2), lake (2), and bifurcation (3). Sweat pores (4) are part of level 3 features. Levels 2 & 3 traits are essential for fingerprint discovery since they serve as fingerprint features. To identify people with damaged fingerprints, sweat pores (4) can serve as valuable evidence [7,8]. Fig. 12 (a) and (b) show the fingerprint under brightfield and 405 nm UV light, respectively. Fig. 12 (c) shows detailed fingerprint classification. Nanophosphors exhibit excellent adherence to finger-mark surfaces because of their tiny particle size. As it is well known, the larger the degree of porosity, the stronger the forces of adhesion and the higher the passage of nanophosphors into the substrate. This can be attributed to the porous character of the prepared samples. As a result, it is established that the CDs @ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposites are effective in developing latent fingerprints.

This work describes the successful fabrication of blue-emitting YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphors via the sol-gel method and N-doped CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposites through hydrothermal technique. By combining N-doped CDs with YTWO: Ce3+ nanoparticles to produce N-doped CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanocomposites, we came up with a straightforward method to improve the luminescence properties of these nanoparticles. The morphology is not significantly changed by the inclusion of CDs with YTWO: Ce3+, although the emission intensity of N-doped CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ improved 342.82-fold. At 431 nm, the N-doped CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor emits an intense, broad blue light. The energy transfer (FRET) between N-doped CDs and luminous Ce3+ could serve as the mechanism for luminescence enhancement. Additionally, the composite materials' broad excitation band peaks at 355 nm, analogous to the LED's primary emission spectrum. For optical thermometry or temperature sensing applications, the N-doped CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor showed an excellent Sr value of 12 × 10−2 %K−1 which was calculated using Ea value of 0.22 eV which shows high relative sensitivity than other materials as shown in Table 2. The LFPs images are illustrated by the N-doped CDs@ YTWO: Ce3+ nanophosphor on a non-porous surface. The latent fingerprint's level 1–3 characteristics are easily recognised. After comparing the PL properties with literature reported as shown in Table 1, our synthesised materials have great enhancement in photoluminescence properties with other potential applications in many fields, such as, temperature sensing, latent fingerprint detection, W-LEDs, and plant-growth light.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings can be made available on request to the corresponding author.
